law. A Will has to be attested by two witnesses, and either of the 
two attesting witnesses have to be examined by the propounder 
of the will. In the present matter, we have carefully perused the 
Trial Court’s judgment. There is not an iota of discussion about 
the validity of the Will as contemplated under Section 63 of the 
Succession Act, 1925 and Section 68 of the Evidence Act, 1872 
and yet, the validity of the Will has been upheld. This is contrary 
to law. Even the High Court, while evaluating the validity of the 
Will, has gone on a different tangent and has erroneously held 
that the requirement of examining the attesting witnesses springs 
into action only in cases of disputes between legal heirs. Such an 
358 
Supreme Court Reports
observation is quite contrary to law, for Section 68 of the Evidence 
Act makes it mandatory to examine at least one of the attesting 
witnesses of the Will. Mere fact that the Will was registered will not
